steps.text: Check for potentially combustible dusts on site.
Determine if combustibility or explosibility tests have been done.
Assess if the dusts are combustible or explosive.
Follow NFPA 652.
Determine if the dusts are from agricultural or food processing.
Determine if the dusts are metals or metal alloys.
Determine if the dust is sulfur.
Determine if the dusts are from woodworking or wood processing.
Check if a Dust Hazard Analysis (DHA) has been performed.
Check if the DHA has been implemented in accordance with regulations and standards.
Follow NFPA 61 and 652.
Follow NFPA 484.
Follow NFPA 655 and 652.
Follow NFPA 664 and 652.
Follow NFPA 652 and 654.
Make necessary corrections.
Document observations.
Take photos and measurements.
Request, collect and study provided documents.
Draw pictures and write notes.
Conduct interviews.
Evaluate the evidence collected.
Involve others in your inspection as early as possible when assistance is needed.
Consider how all four or five elements of a combustible dust explosion are or could be met.
Determine where samples should be taken.
Take dust accumulation measurements.
Collect adequate quantities of combustible dust samples and perform additional walk-arounds as needed.
Confirm dust tests to request from the SLTC Lab.
Safely handle and ship samples to the SLTC Lab.
Review information collected.
Obtain a better understanding of industry, process, and equipment hazards (if necessary).
Inspect, test, and maintain combustible dust-handling and dust-related equipment and systems in accordance with industry standards.
Ask managers, engineers, and maintenance workers for a copy of maintenance records.
Request a copy of the ignition control program from managers and employees.
Ensure electrical equipment and devices are intrinsically safe, approved for, or safe for Class II locations.
Post 'No Smoking' signs in areas where smoking is prohibited.
Implement a hot work permit program if hot work is done.
Utilize grounding and bonding, and other methods, for dissipating any electrostatic charge that could be generated.
Select and use powered industrial trucks that are approved for the combustible dust locations.
Ground personnel who perform manual emptying or filling of containers or vessels during these operations.
Ask managers and engineers for a copy of the bill of materials.
Ensure combustible dust handling equipment and systems are constructed of non-combustible materials.
Operate facilities, equipment, and systems to prevent fires, deflagrations, and explosions.
Ask managers and employees for a copy of the written procedures and safe work practices.
Review upstream equipment, ask questions, identify markings, and obtain/review manuals
Review replacement filters in stock, ask questions, and identify markings.
Ask questions, and obtain/review the manual for filter cleaning mechanism.
Measure, obtain/review the manual, contact the manufacturer, and review applicable NFPA standards.
Review AMS design and field installation.
Identify markings, contact the manufacturer, and review applicable NFPA standards.
Avoid the formation of a dust cloud during the sampling activity.
Utilize bonding and grounding procedures.
Wear PPE including hard hat, safety glasses, and FR clothing.
Ensure that the dust collection and hopper transfer systems are adequately controlled by the employer prior to performing the sampling.
Carefully scoop the combustible dust from the collection drum with the aluminum, conductive hand scoop.
Transfer the contents of the scoop into a 1-liter sampling container.
Repeat until the 1-liter sampling container is nearly full. Close the lid tightly and wipe off excess dust that may be clinging to the bottle.
Apply the sample seal.
Remove the heel grounders and bonding wires.
Notify the employer that sampling is complete.
Don the heel grounders prior to sampling.
Connect the hand activated clamp end of the scoop bonding wire to a nearby grounded metal surface.
Visually verify that the grounding clamps contact clean metal surfaces.
Verify grounding path based on observation of the structural steel or metal equipment, and ground.
Attach the thumb screw end of the scoop bonding wire to the handle of the aluminum, conductive hand scoop.
Check that all systems are visually bonded.
Utilize a natural bristle brush or your clean hand to gently sweep the dust into the aluminum, conductive hand scoop.
Be cautious and avoid the use of excessive force or speed across the surface upon which the dust has accumulated.
Avoid generating a dust cloud.
Notify the employer that sampling at that location has been completed.
Prior to sampling, always ensure that open areas inside of containers and equipment of combustible metal dusts are well ventilated.
Do not sample metal dusts that are in non-metallic or non-conductive containers.
If the metal dust does not have a low-MIE, follow the Procedure for Safe Sampling of Most Combustible Dusts.

If sampling low-MIE metal dusts from, or on, conductive containers or surfaces, follow the procedure for Sampling Low-MIE Dusts from Metal Collection Drums, or Sampling Low-MIE Dusts from Horizontal or Vertical Surfaces.
If sampling low-MIE metal dusts from inside equipment, follow the procedure for Sampling Low-MIE Dusts from Inside Equipment.
Sampling should not be done until approved by the AO and RO.

preconditions.text: If there are any questions as to where it is best to take a representative sample for a given piece of equipment, contact your Regional Office for guidance.
If there is a question about requirements for dust collectors, controls, or sampling procedures, contact your supervisor.
Before sampling, ensure that the dust collection and hopper transfer systems are adequately controlled.
Before sampling, don the heel grounders.

constraints.text: CSHO safety during sampling is paramount.
Ensure personnel bonding and grounding when handling low MIE dusts.
Wear PPE including hard hat, safety glasses, and FR clothing.
Be cautious to avoid generating a dust cloud during sampling.
If possible, avoid leaning into the drum with the upper torso and face during sampling.
Potential ignition source mechanism(s) includes electrostatic build-up/discharge.
Frictional heating and/or sparking from introduction of foreign materials (e.g., tramp metal, or stones).
Overheating of internal material from improper operation.
